answer:
Yes — with an important caveat: research does **not** say “interesting work doesn’t matter.” It says something closer to:

> **Broad interest fit is a real but surprisingly modest predictor of job satisfaction; once a job is above some threshold of meaningful/engaging, factors like autonomy, manager quality, social environment, workload, growth, and role fit often matter as much or more.**

So “pick the most intellectually interesting job possible” is probably too strong as a general rule.

Some relevant results:

### 1. Vocational “interest fit” predicts satisfaction, but not hugely

Meta-analyses on person–vocation or interest congruence — e.g. whether someone’s occupational interests match their job — generally find correlations with job satisfaction around **r ≈ .15–.25**. That is real, but not enormous: it explains only a few percent of the variance in job satisfaction.

Classic/meta-analytic work includes Assouline & Meir 1987, Tranberg et al. 1993, and later vocational-interest meta-analyses. The rough takeaway is: people are somewhat more satisfied when their work matches their interests, but it is not the dominant determinant of happiness.

This is especially relevant to your question: if both jobs are already in the frontier AI/research-engineering universe, the marginal difference in “interestingness” may not translate into a huge happiness difference.

### 2. Broader person–job fit matters more than “topic interest”

A major meta-analysis by Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction — roughly **r ≈ .5** in some estimates.

But “person–job fit” is broader than “I find the topic fascinating.” It includes things like:

- Do my skills match the role?
- Do I have the kind of autonomy I want?
- Does the job use my strengths?
- Is the pace sustainable?
- Do I like the people I work with?
- Does the role match the identity I want to grow into?

So your desire to lead a small team is not a side issue. It may be a major component of fit.

### 3. Work design and day-to-day conditions are very predictive

Work-design research, including Hackman & Oldham’s job characteristics model and later meta-analyses such as Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, suggests that satisfaction is strongly affected by things like:

- Autonomy
- Feedback
- Task significance
- Skill variety
- Social support
- Clarity of role
- Quality of supervision

A “less interesting” project with high autonomy, excellent colleagues, real ownership, and a good manager can easily beat a fascinating project embedded in frustrating org dynamics.

### 4. Job satisfaction affects life satisfaction, but it is not all of life

Meta-analyses such as Bowling et al. 2010 find a moderate-to-strong relation between job satisfaction and life satisfaction, often around **r ≈ .4–.5**. Work matters a lot.

But that also means that even meaningful differences in job satisfaction are only one component of overall happiness. Sleep, relationships, stress, commute, health, autonomy, and financial security all matter too.

### 5. For high earners, “a bit more salary” may matter less than role trajectory

Income does correlate with well-being, and newer work suggests the relationship may continue at high incomes more than older “plateau” claims implied. But the effect is roughly logarithmic: an extra 10–20% matters less when you already have a high tech/research salary than it would earlier in your career.

So I would probably not make the decision mainly on “a bit higher salary,” unless the difference meaningfully changes your savings rate, risk tolerance, family plans, or ability to buy freedom later.

---

## Applying this to your Meta vs Anthropic situation

I’d distinguish between two meanings of “interesting”:

1. **Topic-interest:** “Is the technical/research problem intrinsically fascinating?”
2. **Role-interest:** “Is the shape of the job what I want — leading, setting direction, mentoring, having leverage?”

You may be underrating the second. If you have “always wanted” to lead a small team, the Anthropic role may actually be more interesting along an important axis, even if the technical problem is less exciting.

The key question is probably not:

> “Should I choose interesting research or leadership?”

It is more like:

> “Do I want to optimize for deep technical exploration right now, or for becoming a research/engineering leader?”

Those are both legitimate, high-upside paths.

---

## A useful decision heuristic

I’d rate each role from 1–10 on:

- Intellectual excitement
- Autonomy
- Manager quality
- Team quality
- Mission/values fit
- Learning rate
- Career capital
- Ability to shape the work
- Stress/sustainability
- Compensation
- Reversibility — can I go back to IC/research later?
- Identity fit — does this make me more the person I want to become?

Then ask: which factors are below threshold?

For example:

- If Anthropic is “less interesting” but still a 7/10 technically, while leadership is a 9/10 and autonomy/team are strong, that could be a great move.
- If Anthropic is more like 3–4/10 technically and you expect to feel bored or detached, I’d be cautious. Research motivation compounds; boredom can hurt both happiness and output.
- If Meta’s work is uniquely exciting and rare, and you can plausibly get leadership opportunities there within 6–18 months, staying may be better.
- If team leadership is a scarce opportunity and you’re excited by Anthropic’s mission, taking it may be worth the technical-interest tradeoff.

---

## My read from the literature

The research does support skepticism toward the slogan “just do the most interesting work.” Interest matters, but it is not overwhelmingly predictive of happiness. A better rule is:

> **Choose work that is sufficiently interesting, strongly aligned with your desired role/identity, high in autonomy, with good people and a sustainable environment.**

In your case, I would not dismiss the Anthropic offer just because the project is less interesting. The leadership opportunity may be a major source of satisfaction and long-term growth. But I also would not take it merely for slightly higher salary. The crux is whether the leadership path at Anthropic feels genuinely energizing and whether the technical work remains above your minimum excitement threshold.

answer:
Short answer: **yes-ish**—the research does *not* support the simple advice “maximize how interesting the work is.” It supports a weaker claim: **interest fit matters, but only moderately, and other features of the job often matter as much or more**—autonomy, manager/team quality, stress, meaning, growth, and day-to-day social environment.

### What the research roughly says

1. **Interest-job fit predicts job satisfaction, but modestly**
   - Meta-analyses of vocational interest fit—e.g. Holland-style “does this job match my interests?”—usually find correlations with job satisfaction around **r ≈ .20–.30**, depending on measurement.
   - That is real, but not overwhelming. In plain terms: people whose jobs match their interests are more satisfied on average, but interest fit explains only a modest share of the variance.
   - See e.g. Hoff et al., *Journal of Vocational Behavior*, 2020; Nye et al., 2012.

2. **Broader person-job fit matters more than “interesting topic”**
   - Person-job fit includes not only interest, but also abilities, values, working style, desired autonomy, and rewards.
   - Meta-analyses find broader fit relates more strongly to satisfaction than narrow interest congruence.
   - So “this research problem is fascinating” is only one component of fit.

3. **Autonomy, competence, relatedness, and meaning are very robust predictors**
   - Self-determination theory and work-design research repeatedly find that people are happier and more motivated when they have:
     - autonomy/control,
     - a sense of competence/mastery,
     - good relationships,
     - meaningful impact,
     - clear feedback.
   - In your case, “leading a small team” may increase autonomy, competence, status, mentorship, and impact—even if the technical topic is less intrinsically exciting.

4. **People often overweight salient differences when making decisions**
   - Affective forecasting and the “focusing illusion” literature suggest that when deciding, people overfocus on the attribute currently under comparison—salary, prestige, interestingness, etc.—and underweight the full lived experience of a normal week.
   - A job that is “less interesting” on paper but gives you leadership, ownership, great coworkers, and a healthier operating environment may feel better day-to-day than expected.

5. **Money helps, but with diminishing returns**
   - At high compensation levels, a “bit higher salary” is nice but usually not life-changing unless it changes your financial security, family options, housing, or runway.
   - The marginal happiness gain from salary is generally smaller than the effect of bad management, lack of autonomy, chronic stress, or doing work you find pointless.

### How I’d apply this to your situation

I would not ask:

> “Which job is more intellectually interesting?”

I would ask:

> “Which job gives me the better whole life and career trajectory over the next 2–3 years?”

For you, the key tradeoff seems to be:

- **Meta:** more interesting individual research / technical work.
- **Anthropic:** slightly more money, team leadership, possibly more ownership, but less interesting content.

The leadership piece is important. If leading a small team is something you’ve “always wanted,” that is not a minor perk. It may represent a transition into a role shape you value more: mentoring, setting direction, multiplying others, having broader influence. The research would absolutely count that as a major source of satisfaction if it fits your personality.

But the warning is: **team lead roles often contain much less of the fun technical work than people expect**. More meetings, alignment, performance issues, roadmap ambiguity, recruiting, stakeholder management. If the technical area is already “much less interesting,” and the management work turns out to be annoying rather than energizing, that could be a bad combination.

### A practical decision test

Try to estimate your actual weekly experience.

For each role, write a realistic calendar:

- hours of deep technical work,
- meetings,
- people management,
- roadmap/planning,
- writing/reviewing,
- organizational politics,
- mentoring,
- time with people you respect,
- work you find meaningful.

Then rate each role on:

1. **Interest:** Will I care about the work after 12 months?
2. **Autonomy:** Will I have real control?
3. **Growth:** Am I building the next version of myself?
4. **People:** Do I like/respect my manager and team?
5. **Energy:** Will the average week drain me or energize me?
6. **Reversibility:** If wrong, can I return to a similar IC/research role?
7. **Regret:** Which missed opportunity would bother me more?

### My read

The evidence suggests you should **not automatically stay at Meta just because the work is more interesting**. “Most interesting topic” is not reliably happiness-maximizing.

But you also should **not dismiss interestingness**. If the Anthropic work is merely “less exciting but still meaningful,” the leadership opportunity may be worth a lot. If it is “I’ll be bored and detached from the core work,” that is a serious red flag.

A good heuristic:

- If Anthropic is **7/10 interesting** and gives you real leadership/autonomy → strongly consider it.
- If Anthropic is **3–4/10 interesting** and leadership is mostly coordination/glue work → be cautious.
- If Meta can offer a credible path to leading people while keeping you on fascinating work → that may dominate both.

So the research-backed answer is: **interesting work matters, but it is one ingredient, not the master variable.** For long-run happiness, “good fit + autonomy + growth + people + meaning” usually beats “maximally interesting technical problem” alone.

That means interest-fit is real, but it does **not** explain most of the variance in job satisfaction. A job being intellectually fascinating helps, but it is far from the only thing.

Classic references: Spokane 1985; Assouline & Meir 1987; later meta-analytic work on vocational interests and work outcomes.

### 2. Broader job design factors often matter more

Meta-analyses of job characteristics find that satisfaction is strongly related to things like:

- **Autonomy**
- **Skill variety**
- **Feedback**
- **Task significance**
- **Social support**
- **Good manager / low politics**
- **Reasonable workload**
- **Opportunity to grow**

For example, the Job Characteristics Model literature and later meta-analyses, e.g. Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, generally find that autonomy and social support are among the strongest predictors of satisfaction.

So a less “interesting” technical area can still be more satisfying if it gives you more agency, ownership, leadership, impact, and good collaborators.

### 3. People adapt to many job changes

There is a “honeymoon–hangover” effect in job changes: people often get a satisfaction boost after moving jobs, but some of that fades over time. Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy 2005 is one well-known paper on this.

This does not mean job changes do not matter. They do. But it suggests that the shiny parts of a new role—salary bump, title, brand, novelty—may matter less after 6–18 months than the recurring daily experience.

### 4. People often mispredict what will affect happiness

Affective forecasting research shows that people tend to overestimate the long-run happiness impact of salient features. Daniel Gilbert, Tim Wilson, Daniel Kahneman and others have written about this. Kahneman’s “focusing illusion” is relevant:

> “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.”

When choosing jobs, people may overfocus on the abstract label—“this research topic is fascinating” or “this company is more prestigious”—and underweight daily factors like meetings, manager quality, interruptions, pressure, and whether they feel effective.

## Applying this to your Meta vs Anthropic choice

I would not frame the decision as:

> interesting work vs money/management

I would frame it as:

> Which role gives me the best expected day-to-day life plus long-term growth?

The Anthropic role may be “less interesting” in the narrow research-topic sense, but “leading a small team” could be deeply interesting in another sense: mentoring, setting direction, having leverage, building taste as a leader, making tradeoffs, and owning outcomes.

The salary bump probably should not dominate the decision, especially if it is only “a bit higher” and you are already highly compensated. At your income level, more salary is nice, but it is unlikely to be the main driver of happiness unless it materially changes savings, risk tolerance, or family options.

The key question is whether the Anthropic role is less interesting like:

> “The research topic is somewhat less exciting, but I would have more ownership, leadership, and mission alignment.”

or less interesting like:

> “I would be bored, less intellectually alive, and mostly doing coordination work I do not enjoy.”

Those are very different.

## A practical decision test

Before deciding, I would try to answer these questions concretely.

### Ask Anthropic

1. **What would my calendar look like in a typical week?**
   - 20% research / 80% management?
   - 50/50?
   - Mostly meetings and coordination?

2. **What decisions would I actually own?**
   - Roadmap?
   - Hiring?
   - Technical direction?
   - Performance reviews?
   - Cross-org alignment?

3. **Who is my manager, and how much autonomy would I have?**

4. **What would success look like after 6 and 12 months?**

5. **How intense is the role really?**
   - Hours?
   - Urgency?
   - On-call / incident-style pressure?
   - Emotional load of management?

6. **Can I shape the role toward problems I find more interesting?**

### Ask yourself

Rate each option 1–10 on:

- Intellectual curiosity
- Autonomy
- Learning rate
- Impact
- Quality of people around you
- Manager quality
- Stress / sustainability
- Career capital
- Alignment with future self
- Excitement to wake up and do the work

Then look not only at the total score, but at the **dealbreakers**. A job with low intellectual interest but high autonomy may be great. A job with low interest and low autonomy is dangerous.

## My read from what you wrote

The sentence that stands out is:

> “Leading a small team is something I always wanted to do.”

That is important. If you genuinely want to become a leader, then Anthropic may offer a scarce experiment: you get to test whether management/leadership is a source of meaning for you. That could be worth more than incremental research interestingness.

But if your current Meta work is unusually energizing and Anthropic’s work feels meaningfully dull, I would be cautious. Being a first-time manager in a less-interesting area can turn into: fewer technical highs, more meetings, more ambiguity, more people problems.

So my tentative advice would be:

> **Do not optimize for “most interesting research topic.” Optimize for a role that gives you enough intellectual interest plus high autonomy, growth, people quality, and alignment with the person you want to become.**

If Anthropic gives you real ownership and a credible path into leadership, it could be the better choice despite being less technically interesting. If it mainly gives you a title and compensation bump while reducing your day-to-day energy, staying at Meta—or negotiating for team leadership internally—may be better.

answer:
Yes. The short version is: **“interesting work” matters, but the evidence does not support optimizing your career around “the most intellectually interesting topic possible” as if that dominates everything else.** In occupational psychology, *interest fit* is a real predictor of satisfaction, but usually a modest one; autonomy, good colleagues/manager, workload, agency, meaning, growth, and day-to-day work design often matter as much or more.

A few relevant findings:

- **Vocational interest fit predicts job satisfaction, but modestly.** Meta-analyses around Holland/RIASEC “person–environment fit” and later work on interest fit generally find correlations in the rough range of **r ≈ .20–.30** with job satisfaction, depending on measurement. That is meaningful, but it explains only a single-digit-ish percentage of variance. See e.g. Spokane, Meir & Catalano 2000; Hoff et al. 2020 on interest fit and job satisfaction.

- **Broader person–job fit matters more than “topic interest.”** When fit includes things like values, abilities, needs, autonomy, rewards, and work style—not just whether the subject matter is fascinating—the association with satisfaction is stronger. Kristof-Brown et al. 2005 found broad person–job/person–organization fit to be substantially related to job satisfaction.

- **Work design variables are very important.** The job characteristics literature—Hackman & Oldham; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007—finds that autonomy, task significance, feedback, skill variety, social support, and manageable demands are strongly associated with satisfaction and motivation. For many people, “I control important work with competent people” beats “the topic is maximally interesting but my day-to-day is frustrating.”

- **Salary increases have limited marginal happiness effects at high incomes.** Pay matters, especially for security, fairness, status, and optionality. But at Meta/Anthropic compensation levels, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely to be the main driver of daily happiness unless it changes your life materially. Judge et al. 2010 found pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction.

- **People adapt and mispredict.** Affective forecasting research suggests people often overestimate how much a shiny new condition—more money, prestige, a fascinating project—will affect long-run happiness. Daily recurring features like meetings, stress, manager quality, ownership, and commute/workload tend to matter more than imagined.

So if you are comparing:

> Meta: very interesting research/engineering work  
> Anthropic: somewhat less interesting content, higher pay, small-team leadership you’ve wanted

I would not frame it as “interesting work versus money.” The more important question may be:

**Which role gives you the better day-to-day mix of autonomy, growth, impact, people, and sustainable energy?**

Leading a small team is not just an extrinsic perk. It may satisfy deep intrinsic motives: agency, mentorship, ownership, leverage, status, broader impact, and learning a new craft. If you genuinely want to become a technical lead/manager, that could be more motivating over the next 2–3 years than staying on the most interesting technical problem.

A useful decision test:

1. **If the salary were identical, would Anthropic still be tempting?**  
   If yes, the leadership opportunity is real. If no, maybe you are being pulled by compensation/prestige.

2. **If Meta offered you a small-team lead role in 6–12 months, would you stay?**  
   If yes, maybe first try to create that path internally.

3. **How boring is “less interesting”?**  
   There is a big difference between “not my absolute favorite research agenda” and “I will feel dead inside.”

4. **What will your calendar look like after 6 months?**  
   Estimate percentages: deep technical work, mentoring, meetings, planning, stakeholder management, hiring, incident response, writing, evaluation, politics. Happiness often follows the calendar.

5. **Do you like the Anthropic manager and team?**  
   For job satisfaction, this may matter more than the abstract company/project comparison.

My practical take: **do not choose the “most interesting” work by default.** Choose the role with the best expected lived experience and growth trajectory. If Anthropic gives you real leadership, good autonomy, strong people, acceptable technical engagement, and a mission you respect, it could very plausibly make you happier even if the research topic is less intrinsically exciting. But if the work is genuinely dull to you and the leadership scope is vague or mostly coordination without agency, staying at Meta or negotiating for a lead path there may be better.

The research-backed conclusion is: **interest matters, but it is not a trump card.** For high-skill research/engineering careers, “interesting enough + high agency + great people + growth” often beats “maximally interesting + lower agency.”

answer:
Yes — the evidence broadly says **“interesting work matters, but it is not the whole story, and maximizing ‘topic interestingness’ is probably not the same as maximizing happiness.”** The strongest version of “do the most interesting thing possible” is not well-supported.

A few relevant findings:

### 1. Interest fit predicts satisfaction, but only modestly

There’s a large vocational-psychology literature on **person–environment fit** / **interest congruence**: e.g. whether someone with investigative/scientific interests is in an investigative/scientific job.

Meta-analyses generally find that interest congruence is positively related to job satisfaction, but not overwhelmingly so — often correlations around **~0.15–0.30**, depending on measures and study design.

Relevant reviews/meta-analyses include:

- Assouline & Meir, 1987  
- Spokane, Meir & Catalano, 2000  
- Tsabari, Tziner & Meir, 2005  
- Nye et al., 2012 on vocational interests and performance/persistence

Interpretation: being in a job that fits your interests helps, but it explains only a minority of the variance in satisfaction. It is not like “maximum interest = maximum happiness.”

### 2. Broader job design factors often matter as much or more

The work-design literature finds strong links between job satisfaction and things like:

- autonomy  
- skill variety  
- feedback  
- task significance  
- social support  
- quality of management  
- sense of competence/progress  
- low unnecessary bureaucracy  
- reasonable stress/load

Classic and meta-analytic references:

- Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model  
- Fried & Ferris, 1987  
- Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007

In practice, an “interesting” research problem with low autonomy, unclear goals, political friction, bad management, or no visible progress can be less satisfying than a less intellectually dazzling role with more ownership, impact, and good collaborators.

### 3. People overweight salient job attributes when forecasting happiness

There’s also affective-forecasting research showing that people often overestimate how much a single salient attribute will affect their future happiness. Kahneman and others call this the **focusing illusion**: when you are actively comparing jobs, the differentiating feature — e.g. “this research is cooler” or “this role lets me manage” — looms larger than it will in daily life.

Relevant names:

- Daniel Kahneman  
- Daniel Gilbert  
- Timothy Wilson  
- Schkade & Kahneman, 1998

For job decisions, the practical implication is: don’t evaluate the offer as an abstract bundle of prestige/topic/salary. Evaluate the **actual week-to-week experience**.

### 4. Salary increases usually have weak effects, unless the difference changes your life

Pay matters, especially if it affects security, lifestyle, family obligations, or perceived fairness. But beyond that, pay has a weaker relationship with job satisfaction than people often expect. A meta-analysis by Judge et al., 2010 found a relatively modest relationship between pay level and job satisfaction.

So “a bit higher salary” should probably be a tiebreaker, not the core reason.

### 5. Job changes often have a honeymoon effect

Research on job transitions finds that people often get a satisfaction boost after moving, but some of that fades — the “honeymoon-hangover” pattern. So the Anthropic offer may feel exciting partly because it is new and agentic, while your Meta role may feel more ordinary because you are already there.

That doesn’t mean the offer is bad; it just means you should try to distinguish:

- excitement about novelty  
- excitement about leadership  
- excitement about Anthropic specifically  
- dissatisfaction or stagnation at Meta  
- actual expected daily work

---

## How I’d apply this to your situation

The key question is probably not:

> “Which job has the most interesting research?”

It is more like:

> “Which job gives me more good weeks over the next 2–3 years?”

For someone in your position, I’d break it down like this:

### Reasons to stay at Meta

Stay if most of these are true:

- The research is genuinely rare and hard to replicate elsewhere.
- You are still learning fast.
- You have strong collaborators and high agency.
- You would be sad to give up the technical direction.
- The Anthropic role is not merely “less interesting,” but meaningfully uninspiring.
- You suspect you want leadership in the abstract but may not enjoy the actual calendar of a lead/manager.

### Reasons to join Anthropic

Join if most of these are true:

- Leading a small team is not just status/compensation — it is something you want to practice daily.
- The work is “less interesting” technically, but still above your threshold for caring.
- The team quality, mission, and execution environment are excellent.
- You would gain career capital you cannot easily get at Meta soon.
- You are feeling a pull toward ownership, mentorship, strategy, and leverage rather than only technical depth.
- The Anthropic role gives you a plausible path back into more interesting technical work later.

A big distinction:  
**“Less interesting” is fine. “I won’t care about this” is dangerous.**

You do not need the maximally interesting job. But you probably do need a job where you can feel some combination of curiosity, ownership, progress, and meaning.

---

## A practical test

Ask Anthropic for a very concrete picture of the role:

- What would my calendar look like in a normal week?
- How much IC research vs team leadership?
- What are the top three problems the team is solving?
- What decisions would I own?
- What would success look like after 6 and 18 months?
- Who would I report to, and how are priorities set?
- How much freedom would I have to shape the technical agenda?
- If I later wanted to return toward more hands-on research, is that realistic?

Then compare that to your current Meta week, not your idealized Meta project.

A useful exercise: write two fake diary entries.

1. **A Tuesday at Meta, 12 months from now.**
2. **A Tuesday at Anthropic, 12 months from now.**

Include meetings, code/research time, politics, mentoring, reading, writing, stress, commute, manager interactions, and how you feel at 7pm. This is often more predictive than asking “which opportunity is more interesting?”

---

## Bottom line

The research supports a moderate version of “do interesting work”:

> Interest fit matters, but it is only one contributor to job satisfaction. Autonomy, progress, social environment, competence, meaning, and day-to-day work design often matter just as much or more.

So if Anthropic is **only slightly less interesting** but gives you leadership, ownership, good people, and strong future optionality, it could easily make you as happy or happier.

If it is **much less interesting below your caring threshold**, the salary and title probably will not compensate for that for long.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there’s evidence that “interesting work” matters, but it is not usually the dominant or sufficient predictor of happiness/job satisfaction**. The stronger finding is: people are happiest when the job gives them a good mix of **autonomy, competence/growth, relatedness, meaning, reasonable stress, good management, and fit**. “Most intellectually interesting topic” is only one part of that.

I would not read the literature as saying “interestingness doesn’t matter.” Especially in research, it can matter a lot. But I would read it as saying: **do not optimize solely for the most interesting technical problem.**

### What the research roughly says

#### 1. Interest fit predicts satisfaction, but only modestly

Vocational psychology has studied “interest congruence”: do people whose interests match their jobs end up more satisfied? The answer is yes, but the effect is usually **small to moderate**, not overwhelming. Meta-analyses of Holland-style interest fit often find correlations with job satisfaction in the rough range of **r ≈ .10–.30** depending on methods and samples.

Translation: if your job is wildly misaligned with your interests, that is bad. But among reasonably good jobs, “this topic is 20% more interesting” may not move happiness as much as you expect.

#### 2. Work design variables are often stronger predictors

Classic job-design research — Hackman & Oldham, and later meta-analyses like Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007 — finds that things like:

- autonomy,
- feedback,
- task significance,
- skill variety,
- social support,
- clarity,
- reasonable workload,

are strongly associated with job satisfaction and motivation.

This is relevant because the Anthropic role may score lower on “technical interestingness” but higher on:

- autonomy,
- responsibility,
- leadership,
- impact,
- social connection,
- growth into management.

Those can be very real sources of satisfaction.

#### 3. Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness

A large body of work under self-determination theory says people do better when work satisfies three psychological needs:

1. **Autonomy** — I have real agency.
2. **Competence** — I am growing and effective.
3. **Relatedness** — I feel connected to people I respect.

“Interesting research” often gives competence and intrinsic motivation. “Leading a small team” can give autonomy, relatedness, and impact. The key question is which needs are currently under-satisfied for you.

#### 4. Salary matters, but probably less at your margin

Pay is not irrelevant, but job-satisfaction meta-analyses generally find pay level has a weaker relationship with satisfaction than many people expect. At Meta/Anthropic compensation levels, unless the difference changes your financial freedom meaningfully, the salary bump is probably not the central variable.

The exception: if the higher salary changes your sense of security, optionality, ability to support family, etc., then it may matter more than “salary” as a number.

#### 5. Job switches often have a honeymoon effect

There is research on the “honeymoon-hangover” pattern after job changes: people often feel a burst of satisfaction after switching, then partially adapt. So be careful not to overweight:

- novelty,
- title,
- prestige,
- higher comp,
- “finally I’ll lead a team.”

Ask instead: **what will my average Tuesday feel like 12 months in?**

### The research-specific caveat

For research roles, “interestingness” may matter more than it does in average job-satisfaction studies. If the work is genuinely intellectually alive for you, that can drive:

- creativity,
- persistence,
- taste,
- willingness to read deeply,
- spontaneous thinking outside work,
- better research judgment.

Amabile’s work on creativity and intrinsic motivation is relevant here: people tend to be more creative when they are intrinsically motivated by the work itself.

So I would not dismiss your current Meta role being “very interesting.” That is a major asset.

But there is another side: management/leadership can itself become the interesting problem. If you have always wanted to lead a small team, the Anthropic role may be less interesting technically but more interesting as a systems/people/strategy problem.

### How I’d frame your decision

I would separate “interestingness” into two questions:

1. **Is the technical topic less interesting, but still above threshold?**  
   If yes, the leadership opportunity may dominate.

2. **Or is it below threshold — i.e. you expect to be bored, disengaged, or not proud of the work?**  
   If yes, beware. Leadership will not fully compensate for disliking the core mission/problem.

A useful test:

> If you removed the salary increase, would you still seriously consider Anthropic for the leadership opportunity?

If yes, that’s a strong signal.  
If no, the offer may be mostly compensation/prestige/title, which are less reliable happiness drivers.

Another test:

> In your current job, what gives you more energy: solving the research problem yourself, or helping other people solve better problems?

If the first, staying technical may be better.  
If the second, the Anthropic role may be a real growth step.

### Questions I would ask Anthropic before deciding

You want to know whether “team lead” is a genuine satisfying leadership role or just more meetings on less interesting work.

Ask:

- What percentage of time will be technical vs managerial?
- What decisions will I actually own?
- Will I manage people formally, or just coordinate?
- What does success look like after 6 and 12 months?
- How strong is the team?
- Who is my manager, and how do they support first-time leads?
- Can I shape the technical roadmap?
- If I discover I prefer IC work, is there a path back?
- Is the less-interesting area strategically important, or peripheral?
- Will I have recruiting/headcount authority?
- What are the expected hours and on-call/incident load?

Also ask yourself: could you get a similar small-team leadership path at Meta while keeping the more interesting work? If yes, that might dominate both options.

### My summary take

The literature supports this view:

> “Choose the job with the best overall motivational ecology, not necessarily the most interesting topic.”

For you, the key tradeoff is not salary vs interestingness. It is:

- **deep technical curiosity and current momentum at Meta**
  
versus

- **leadership, autonomy, growth, and possibly broader impact at Anthropic**.

If Anthropic is only “somewhat less interesting” and the team-lead role is real, I’d take the leadership opportunity very seriously. If it is “much less interesting” in a way that would make your day-to-day feel dull, I’d be cautious — especially because in research, intrinsic interest is not just a perk; it is part of what makes you good.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, sort of**. The research does **not** support “just maximize how interesting the topic is.” It supports a more boring but useful claim: people are happier when work gives them **autonomy, competence/growth, good people/manager, reasonable stress, meaning, and fit**. “Interesting work” is one way to get those, but not the only way.

I don’t know of a clean study where people are randomized into “most interesting possible job” vs “less interesting job.” But the closest literatures are pretty relevant:

### What the evidence roughly says

1. **Vocational interest fit predicts satisfaction, but modestly**
   - Meta-analyses of Holland-style “interest congruence” generally find small-to-moderate correlations with job satisfaction, often around **r ≈ .15–.25**.  
   - That is real, but not huge. It suggests that matching your interests matters, but it does not dominate everything else.

2. **Broader person–job fit matters more**
   - Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson’s 2005 meta-analysis found broad person–job fit is much more strongly associated with job satisfaction — roughly **r ≈ .5** in many analyses.
   - But “fit” includes more than topic interest: values, skills, work style, autonomy, rewards, team, pace, identity, etc.

3. **Job design factors are very important**
   - Work-design meta-analyses, e.g. Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, find that autonomy, task variety, feedback, task significance, social support, etc. are all meaningfully related to satisfaction and motivation.
   - “Interesting technical problems” are partly captured by variety/complexity, but a role with more ownership, leadership, influence, and growth can also score highly on these dimensions.

4. **People adapt to job changes**
   - There is a “honeymoon–hangover” pattern in job satisfaction: people often get a boost after changing jobs, then satisfaction drifts back down as the new situation becomes normal.
   - Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy 2005 is a classic paper on this.
   - So you should be skeptical of both “this new role will make me permanently happier” and “leaving this fascinating role will permanently ruin my happiness.”

5. **People often overforecast the emotional impact of career outcomes**
   - Affective forecasting research, e.g. Gilbert et al. 1998, suggests people overestimate how long and intensely major outcomes will affect happiness. This has included career/tenure-type outcomes.
   - Translation: you may be overestimating how much the interestingness difference will matter, but you may also be overestimating how good “leading a team” will feel.

### How I’d apply this to your Meta vs Anthropic decision

I would not reduce it to “interesting work vs money/title.” The real question is:

> Which role gives you the best expected mix of energy, growth, autonomy, people, mission fit, and future optionality?

In your case, the Anthropic offer has one big non-salary factor: **leading a small team**, which you say you have always wanted. That is not a cosmetic benefit. Leadership can be deeply satisfying if you enjoy mentoring, setting direction, multiplying others, and owning outcomes. It can also be frustrating if what you actually love is deep technical/research flow.

A useful distinction:

- If Anthropic is **less interesting but still intellectually alive**, and gives you real leadership, autonomy, good manager/team, and strong career growth, taking it could be very reasonable.
- If Anthropic is **genuinely boring to you**, and you are mainly tempted by salary/status/“team lead” label, I would be much more cautious.
- If Meta’s work is unusually motivating and you feel you are compounding rare technical capital there, that is a serious reason to stay.

### Questions I’d try to answer before deciding

Ask Anthropic very concretely:

1. **How much of my time is hands-on technical/research work vs people management?**
2. **Do I have real authority over roadmap, hiring, prioritization, and technical direction?**
3. **What makes the work important or intellectually non-obvious?**
4. **Who is my manager, and what is their style?**
5. **What does success look like after 6 and 12 months?**
6. **If I dislike management, can I return to a senior IC/research-engineering track?**
7. **Can the role be shaped as a player-coach role with a meaningful technical agenda?**

Also ask Meta, if appropriate:

- Is there a near-term path to team leadership while staying on interesting work?
- Could you get more formal tech-lead/people-lead scope where you are?

### My read of the research-backed takeaway

The evidence supports this:

> Do not blindly maximize “interesting topic.” Maximize sustainable motivation and fit.

Interestingness matters, especially for research engineers because it affects energy, learning, and performance. But research does **not** suggest that the person who chooses the most intrinsically fascinating job is necessarily much happier than the person who chooses a somewhat less fascinating job with more autonomy, responsibility, growth, and better fit.

So the key is whether the Anthropic role is “less interesting” in a tolerable way, or “less interesting” in a soul-draining way. Those are very different decisions.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, there is evidence against the simple rule “take the most interesting job possible.”** But the stronger conclusion is: **interesting work matters, just not in isolation, and often less than people predict.** The research tends to support optimizing for *day-to-day fit*: autonomy, competence/growth, relationships, manager quality, workload, values, and whether the work uses your preferred mode of operating.

For your Meta vs Anthropic case, the key question may not be “which topic is more interesting?” but: **which job gives you the better daily life and better long-term identity: deep technical IC/researcher vs small-team lead?**

## What the research roughly says

### 1. Interest-fit predicts job satisfaction, but usually only modestly

The closest literature is on **vocational interest congruence** — e.g. Holland-style “this person’s interests match this occupation.” Meta-analyses generally find that interest-fit is positively related to job satisfaction, but the effect is not huge — often correlations around **r ≈ .20**, depending on measures and corrections.

That means interest-fit is real, but it is not destiny. Plenty of people with “matched” jobs are unhappy; plenty with imperfect interest-match are satisfied because the job has autonomy, respect, good colleagues, good management, meaning, or growth.

Relevant work:

- Assouline & Meir, 1987; Spokane, Meir & Catalano, 2000 — person-environment / interest congruence reviews.
- Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005 — broader person-job/person-organization fit meta-analysis; broader fit predicts satisfaction more strongly than narrow interest-fit.

### 2. Broader “job design” factors are very important

A large work-design literature finds that things like:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- social support,
- manageable workload,
- clarity,
- good management,

are robustly related to job satisfaction, motivation, and burnout.

A less intellectually fascinating role with **more ownership, leadership, autonomy, and growth** can easily beat a more fascinating role with worse daily conditions.

Relevant work:

- Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model.
- Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007 meta-analysis on work design.

### 3. Job satisfaction affects life satisfaction, but it is not the whole story

Job satisfaction and overall life satisfaction are moderately related — not independent, but not identical. Your non-work life, health, relationships, personality, commute, stress, sleep, and sense of control all matter a lot.

So even if Job A is “more interesting,” the effect on total happiness may be smaller than expected if Job B gives you more agency, status, growth, money, or better daily social experience.

Relevant work:

- Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989; Bowling et al., 2010 — job satisfaction and subjective well-being meta-analyses.

### 4. People often overpredict the happiness effect of salient job features

Affective forecasting research suggests people overweight the most salient feature of a decision — prestige, salary, intellectual excitement, location, etc. In reality, daily repeated experiences often dominate: who your manager is, whether meetings drain you, whether you have control, whether you feel effective, whether you are learning.

This is especially relevant here: “more interesting research” is salient; “six hours of meetings vs three hours of deep work per day” is what you will actually live.

## Applying this to your situation

I would not frame it as:

> Meta = interesting  
> Anthropic = less interesting but more money/team lead

Instead, decompose “interesting” into several things:

| Dimension | Meta role | Anthropic role |
|---|---:|---:|
| Intellectual excitement of the problem | ? | ? |
| Autonomy / ownership | ? | ? |
| Learning rate | ? | ? |
| Chance to lead | ? | ? |
| Quality of manager | ? | ? |
| Quality of team | ? | ? |
| Daily schedule energy | ? | ? |
| Long-term option value | ? | ? |
| Alignment with identity | ? | ? |
| Compensation / financial goals | ? | ? |

The Anthropic offer has something important: **leading a small team is not merely a perk. It is a different kind of intrinsic motivation.** If you have genuinely wanted to lead, mentor, shape direction, and be accountable for a group’s output, that may be deeply satisfying even if the object-level research is less exciting.

But if what makes you come alive is deep technical problem-solving, experiments, ideas, and uninterrupted research time, then “team lead” may turn into a calendar full of planning, hiring, coordination, 1:1s, prioritization, and conflict resolution. Some people love that. Some people discover they hate it.

## The practical test I’d use

Ask Anthropic for a very concrete picture:

1. **What would my calendar look like in a normal week?**
2. **How much time is IC technical work vs people management vs coordination?**
3. **Do I get real decision authority, or am I just a “lead” without control?**
4. **What are the team’s goals for the next 6–12 months?**
5. **What does success look like?**
6. **Who is my manager, and how are conflicts/priorities handled?**
7. **If I dislike management, can I move back toward IC work?**
8. **Can the research agenda be shaped toward things I find more interesting?**

Also ask Meta, explicitly or implicitly:

> “I’m excited by leadership. Is there a path for me to lead a small team or own a larger research direction here?”

If Meta can give you the interesting work *and* a leadership path, that changes the tradeoff.

## My read of the evidence

The literature does **not** say:

> “Interesting work doesn’t matter.”

It says something more like:

> “Narrow interest-match is only one predictor of happiness. People are often happier when the job gives autonomy, competence, relatedness, meaning, growth, and good daily conditions — even if the nominal topic is not the most interesting possible.”

So if Anthropic is only “less interesting” in the abstract, but gives you leadership, ownership, growth, good people, and mission/impact, it could be the better happiness choice.

If Anthropic is “less interesting” in the concrete daily sense — you would be bored, politically constrained, mostly in meetings you dislike, and away from the research you love — then the pay bump and title probably won’t compensate.

My personal rule of thumb would be:

> Choose the job whose **ordinary Tuesday** you would rather live, not the one whose abstract description sounds best.

And for your specific case: if leading a small team is something you have long wanted to test, and the downside is reversible, the Anthropic role may be a valuable experiment. But I would negotiate hard for clarity on technical scope, autonomy, and the ability to keep doing work you find alive-making.

I’m exploring some patterns in vocational interests and how they relate to job satisfaction. Meta-analyses show a modest link between interest congruence and well-being, with correlations around r≈.15–.30. Interestingly, people in less than perfect job fits can still be quite happy due to factors like management, autonomy, and relationships. Job characteristics such as autonomy and social support have stronger correlations with satisfaction, often between r .30 to .50. If companies like Anthropic provide strong support, they may help offset interest deficits.
**Exploring leadership roles**

I need to avoid saying "Anthropic is less interesting." Instead, it could be that while the mission is strong, a role may lack technical depth. I should stay neutral. It's important to discuss how leading a small team might fulfill a long-held desire, and not dismiss that as just extrinsic motivation. Leadership can be meaningful in itself, leading to opportunities for "job crafting." At Anthropic, this could allow for a more influential role in shaping the research agenda. It might also mean increasing topic interest over time, depending on how the scope can evolve.
**Identifying potential red flags**

I should highlight some important red flags to consider. If a role seems less interesting, it could lead to stagnation, resentment, or a values mismatch. Leadership in a small team without real authority can mean high pressure and lack of publications. If the manager is unknown or has a poor reputation, combined with high turnover, that's concerning. Additionally, if the salary increase is marginal, it might not be worth it. It's crucial to gather information about autonomy and engagement at Anthropic before making a decision—aim for at least a 70-80% intellectual engagement compared to my current position.
answer:
Short answer: yes—there’s evidence that “interest fit” matters, but it usually does **not** look like “choose the maximally interesting job and you’ll be much happier.” The research more strongly suggests: people are happiest when the job satisfies a bundle of needs—autonomy, competence, relationships, manageable stress, values/meaning, good manager, growth, and some interest. Intellectual interestingness is one important input, not the whole objective function.

### What the evidence roughly says

1. **Interest/job fit predicts satisfaction, but modestly.**  
   Meta-analyses on vocational “interest congruence” generally find positive but not huge relationships with job satisfaction/well-being—often in the ballpark of **r ≈ .15–.30**. See e.g. Assouline & Meir 1987; Spokane, Meir & Catalano 2000; Van Iddekinge et al. 2011 / Nye et al. 2012.  
   Translation: doing work aligned with your interests helps, but many people in “less perfectly interesting” jobs are happier than people in “dream-interest” jobs because of other factors.

2. **Broader person–job fit matters more than “interest” alone.**  
   Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 meta-analysis found strong associations between person-job fit and job satisfaction/commitment. But “fit” includes needs, values, skills, rewards, environment—not merely topic interest.

3. **Day-to-day job design is very important.**  
   Work-design meta-analyses, e.g. Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007, show that autonomy, feedback, task variety, task significance, social support, and manageable demands are strongly related to satisfaction and motivation. A small-team lead role could score highly here even if the research topic is less inherently exciting.

4. **People mispredict what will make them happy.**  
   Affective-forecasting research—Gilbert, Wilson and others—suggests people often overestimate how much a single salient feature of a future situation will affect their happiness. “This topic is less interesting” may be salient now, but your actual future experience may be dominated by: your manager, team quality, scope, meetings, stress, autonomy, and whether you feel you’re growing.

5. **New jobs often have a honeymoon/hangover pattern.**  
   Research on job changes, e.g. Boswell et al. 2005, finds that satisfaction often rises after a move and then partially decays. So don’t overvalue the excitement of the offer itself. But if the new role genuinely changes your growth path—e.g. lets you lead—then it may be more than a honeymoon effect.

### How I’d interpret your situation

I would separate “interesting” into at least three things:

1. **Topic interest:** Is the research/problem itself fascinating?
2. **Role interest:** Is the day-to-day work engaging—mentoring, decision-making, technical strategy, team building?
3. **Growth interest:** Does the job move you toward the kind of person/career you want?

It sounds like Meta wins on **topic interest**, while Anthropic may win on **role/growth interest**: higher comp, small-team leadership, more responsibility. That is not obviously “less interesting” overall—it may be less intellectually shiny but more developmentally meaningful.

A good heuristic:

- If Anthropic is only “less interesting” in the sense that the specific research agenda is not your favorite, but the role gives you autonomy, strong teammates, real leadership, and useful career growth, the evidence would not strongly warn against moving.
- If Anthropic is “less interesting” in the sense that you expect to be bored, less technically excellent, more bureaucratic, less autonomous, or misaligned with the work, then the salary bump and title may not compensate.

### Questions I’d try to answer before deciding

Ask Anthropic very concretely:

- What fraction of time is people management vs technical work?
- Will I own research direction, or mostly execute someone else’s roadmap?
- Who are the reports? Are they strong? Do they want this structure?
- What does success look like after 6 and 12 months?
- How much publication/open research/technical exploration is possible?
- How much freedom would I have to make the work more interesting over time?
- Why is this role open? What happened to the previous lead, if any?
- What are the actual weekly meetings, decision rights, and constraints?

And ask yourself:

- Do I want to lead because I enjoy developing people and making teams effective, or because leadership sounds like the next status step?
- Would I be okay doing less hands-on research?
- If I stayed at Meta, could I get a similar leadership opportunity within 6–18 months?
- Which option gives me more optionality two years from now?
- Which failure mode scares me more: being bored at Anthropic, or stagnating as an IC at Meta?

### Bottom line

The literature does **not** support the simplistic advice “maximize interestingness at all costs.” Interest fit matters, but it’s only one contributor to happiness and job satisfaction. Autonomy, growth, competence, social environment, values, stress, and identity can matter as much or more.

So I’d frame the decision not as:

> “Interesting work at Meta vs less interesting work at Anthropic”

but as:

> “Deeper technical fascination at Meta vs broader leadership/growth/autonomy at Anthropic.”

If the Anthropic role is at least moderately interesting and gives you real leadership you’ve wanted, it could be a very reasonable move. If “less interesting” actually means “I will dread the work,” stay at Meta and seek leadership there.

I'm working as a Research Engineer in Meta doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Anthropic - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

People often say that it's super important to do interesting thing in your work, but I feel this might not really be reflected in research. Are there any results showing that people actually are not much happier when they select the most interesting job possible?
